text: Nagel argued that an objective account of the world — the view from nowhere — would, paradoxically, leave out the most fundamental feature of reality: that there is something it is like to be here. This suggests that presence is not a psychological trick but a philosophical commitment. To be present is to affirm that subjective experience has weight, that it is not merely a representation of something more real happening 'underneath' in neurons. The meditator who attends fully to a breath is not evading reality; she is inhabiting the layer of reality that no physical description can substitute for.
question: What does the passage identify as the 'most fundamental feature of reality' that a purely objective account leaves out?

options.text: The neural mechanisms underlying conscious experience
The historical context of subjective philosophical traditions
That there is something it is like to be here — the fact of subjective experience
The gap between philosophical theory and meditative practice
